families.why_it_exists: Play! Pokémon Prize Packs are distributed through Organized Play programs and contain selected cards bearing a Play! Pokémon stamp.
families.why_collectors_care: Prize Pack cards retain original set identity but have Organized Play distribution markings, so they are collected separately from ordinary pack-pulled copies.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the Play! Pokémon stamp and, where present, the specific Prize Pack series marker.
families.grookai_rule: Prize Pack stamps are parent identity lanes; explicit series markers may create separate identity lanes under the Prize Pack governance contract.

families.why_it_exists: Prerelease cards are selected expansion reprints with a Prerelease or expansion-logo stamp; staff versions were produced for event organizers beginning with the Diamond & Pearl expansion.
families.why_collectors_care: Prerelease and Staff stamps identify event-distribution copies, and Staff copies are often more limited because they were distributed to organizers rather than regular participants.
families.how_to_identify: Look for a PRERELEASE, expansion-logo, or STAFF stamp on the card face.
families.grookai_rule: Prerelease and Staff stamps are parent identity modifiers because the stamp changes the distribution identity of the physical card.

families.why_it_exists: δ Delta Species are a distinct kind of Pokémon card first introduced in EX Delta Species, often showing Pokémon with unusual types compared with their normal identity.
families.why_collectors_care: Collectors track Delta Species separately because the δ mark and altered typing define a recognizable EX-era mechanic and theme, not just a finish.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the δ / Delta Species marker and the unusual type assignment on the card.
families.grookai_rule: Delta Species is a parent identity modifier because it changes the printed card identity and search meaning.

families.why_it_exists: Pokémon League and League Challenge events can award special stamped cards, including placement-stamped variants for top finishers.
families.why_collectors_care: Placement stamps encode event achievement or league distribution, making the physical card a different collector object from a normal copy.
families.how_to_identify: Look for a Pokémon League, League Challenge, placement, finalist, or top-cut stamp on the card face.
families.grookai_rule: Event and placement stamps are modeled as parent identity modifiers when the exact event/card pairing is source-backed.

families.why_it_exists: Shiny Vault subsets collect Shiny Pokémon cards under SV-prefixed numbering, including Hidden Fates and Shining Fates.
families.why_collectors_care: SV cards are tracked separately because they are Shiny Pokémon subset cards with their own numbering and checklist identity.
families.how_to_identify: Look for an SV-prefixed card number and Shiny Pokémon artwork/identity.
families.grookai_rule: Shiny Vault cards are parent identity lanes because SV numbering is part of the printed checklist identity.

families.why_it_exists: Trainer Gallery cards are a subset focused on the relationship between Trainer and Pokémon, using TG-prefixed numbering in Sword & Shield expansions.
families.why_collectors_care: TG cards are collected as a separate gallery subset because their numbering, artwork concept, and checklist position are distinct from the main set.
families.how_to_identify: Look for a TG-prefixed card number and Trainer Gallery artwork treatment.
families.grookai_rule: Trainer Gallery is modeled as parent identity, not a finish, because the card belongs to a separate numbered subset.

families.why_it_exists: Radiant Collection is a separately numbered subset that appeared in Legendary Treasures and later Generations, using the RC card-number prefix.
families.why_collectors_care: Collectors track RC cards as a subset lane because their numbering and set membership differ from the main expansion checklist.
families.how_to_identify: Look for an RC-prefixed card number such as RC1, RC10, or RC25.
families.grookai_rule: Subset card-number prefixes are modeled as parent identity lanes when they change checklist membership.

families.why_it_exists: Expansion-logo and set-name stamps are used on selected promotional reprints, including prerelease/event cards and some product or retail promotions tied to a specific expansion.
families.why_collectors_care: The visible set logo or expansion-name stamp changes distribution identity, so collectors track these copies separately from unstamped expansion cards.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the expansion logo or set-name stamp on the artwork area, then verify the exact card against the relevant prerelease, product, or card-specific release source.
families.grookai_rule: Set-logo stamps are parent identity variants. The family-level explanation may be public, but exact campaign/source evidence should remain attached per card where available.

families.why_it_exists: Pokémon LV.X cards were introduced in Diamond & Pearl as Level-Up Pokémon representing a stronger trained state.
families.why_collectors_care: LV.X cards are a distinct era mechanic with unique naming, rules treatment, and checklist identity, so collectors track them apart from ordinary versions of the same Pokémon.
families.how_to_identify: Look for LV.X in the card name and Level-Up styling.
families.grookai_rule: LV.X is modeled as parent identity because it is part of the printed card name/mechanic identity.

families.why_it_exists: Celebrations included a Classic Collection subset of close replicas of popular older cards with special treatment and retained original-style numbering.
families.why_collectors_care: Classic Collection cards are tracked separately from both the main Celebrations set and the original historical cards they reference.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the Celebrations Classic Collection treatment and card identity rather than treating it as the original vintage print.
families.grookai_rule: Classic Collection is a parent identity lane because it is a separate modern subset, not a finish variant of the original card.

families.why_it_exists: WOTC-era production or text/artwork mistakes created repeatable physical variants such as no-damage Ninetales, missing Stage text Blastoise, and corrected/error text lanes.
families.why_collectors_care: Recognized repeatable errors are collected as distinct objects because they document a specific production state rather than ordinary wear or damage.
families.how_to_identify: Compare the affected text, artwork, HP, holo box, set symbol, or other documented error area against the corrected card.
families.grookai_rule: Grookai only models error lanes when they are externally recognized and repeatable at card identity level.

families.why_it_exists: Some Jungle holo rares were printed without the Jungle expansion symbol.
families.why_collectors_care: The missing symbol is a visible, repeatable WOTC-era error that collectors identify separately from normal Jungle holo copies.
families.how_to_identify: Confirm the Jungle holo rare is missing the Jungle set symbol where it should appear.
families.grookai_rule: Recognized repeatable production errors receive parent identity lanes when card-level evidence supports the exact card.

families.why_it_exists: Trick or Trade Halloween mini-set cards are reprints packaged for seasonal Halloween distribution with a Pikachu pumpkin stamp on the card artwork.
families.why_collectors_care: The pumpkin stamp ties the card to the Halloween Trick or Trade product instead of the original expansion pack, making it a separate seasonal collector lane.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the Pikachu jack-o-lantern / pumpkin stamp on the card art and verify the card against the Trick or Trade checklist.
families.grookai_rule: Trick or Trade stamped cards are parent identity variants because the visible seasonal stamp changes distribution identity while preserving the base card identity.

families.why_it_exists: Burger King Pokémon promotions paired selected Diamond & Pearl-era cards with Kids Meal toy campaigns; the 2009 Platinum campaign used visibly stamped reverse-holo cards.
families.why_collectors_care: These cards are tied to a short fast-food promotion and have an identifiable stamp, so collectors track them separately from normal Platinum-era cards.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the Burger King or Platinum promotional stamp on the card face and verify the card against Burger King promo product/checklist sources.
families.grookai_rule: Burger King stamped cards are parent identity variants; the stamped row may then carry its own supported child finish.

families.why_it_exists: Diamond & Pearl and Platinum-era Shiny cards are commonly tracked as an SH-prefixed shiny subset.
families.why_collectors_care: SH cards are sought as shiny variant subset cards and are not ordinary main-set numbering lanes.
families.how_to_identify: Look for an SH-prefixed card number such as SH1 through SH12.
families.grookai_rule: SH-prefixed cards are parent identity lanes because the prefix encodes subset identity.

families.why_it_exists: Build-A-Bear Workshop sold Pokémon plush releases with same-character, Build-A-Bear Workshop-branded Pokémon TCG cards.
families.why_collectors_care: The stamp ties the card to a specific retail plush promotion, making it physically distinguishable from the standard set card and tracked as a separate promotional lane.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the Build-A-Bear Workshop stamp on the card face.
families.grookai_rule: Visible retailer distribution stamps are modeled as parent identity modifiers, not as card finishes.

families.family_label: Call of Legends SL Shiny Secret Subset
families.why_it_exists: Call of Legends included Shiny Secret prints of Legendary Pokémon, commonly identified by SL-prefixed numbering.
families.why_collectors_care: SL cards are collected as a distinct high-interest subset because they are Shiny Secret prints outside the ordinary numbered checklist.
families.how_to_identify: Look for an SL-prefixed card number in Call of Legends.
families.grookai_rule: SL-prefixed cards are parent identity lanes because the prefix is a printed subset identity.

families.why_it_exists: Some older and special sets use nonstandard prefixes or secret numbering lanes that identify cards outside the ordinary numeric checklist.
families.why_collectors_care: The prefix changes checklist identity and helps collectors distinguish secret/subset cards from normal numbered cards.
families.how_to_identify: Check the printed card number prefix and compare it to the set checklist.
families.grookai_rule: Number-prefix identities are parent identity lanes when the prefix changes checklist membership.

families.why_it_exists: Toys R Us released special stamped promotional cards to coincide with Pokémon TCG expansion releases, beginning with the Generations era.
families.why_collectors_care: The Toys R Us stamp identifies a retailer-distribution copy that collectors separate from the ordinary expansion card.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the Toys R Us stamp on the card face.
families.grookai_rule: Retailer-exclusive visible stamps create parent identity lanes when the exact card/stamp pairing is source-backed.

families.why_it_exists: Winner cards are stamped reprints released through Pokémon League or related tournament programs.
families.why_collectors_care: The WINNER stamp marks a tournament/league award copy and is physically distinct from the ordinary card.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the foil WINNER stamp on the card face.
families.grookai_rule: Winner-stamped cards are parent identity variants because the stamp records distribution and award context.

families.why_it_exists: Platinum: Arceus includes a distinct AR-numbered Arceus subset, with individual Arceus cards carrying AR-prefixed card numbers.
families.why_collectors_care: The AR prefix identifies a themed Arceus subset checklist rather than ordinary set numbering.
families.how_to_identify: Look for an AR-prefixed card number such as AR1 through AR9.
families.grookai_rule: AR-prefixed cards are parent identity lanes because the prefix changes checklist membership.

families.why_it_exists: Pokémon TCG Holiday Calendar products include foil cards with festive stamps; older Countdown/Surprise Calendar products used foil snowflake stamps on included cards.
families.why_collectors_care: Snowflake/festive-stamped cards are tied to seasonal calendar products, so collectors track them separately from standard set copies.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the snowflake or festive calendar stamp on the card art and verify the card against the relevant Holiday/Countdown Calendar product list.
families.grookai_rule: Holiday Calendar stamped cards are parent identity variants because the seasonal product stamp changes distribution identity.

families.why_it_exists: W Promotional cards are reprints with a gold foil W stamp.
families.why_collectors_care: The W stamp identifies a separate promotional distribution copy from the normal expansion card.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the gold foil W stamp on the card face.
families.grookai_rule: W-stamped cards are modeled as parent identity variants.

families.why_it_exists: Illustration Rare is a Scarlet & Violet-era rarity outside Asia, used for Full Art Secret cards focused on expanded character or environment artwork.
families.why_collectors_care: Illustration Rares are collected as a modern art-focused rarity lane with distinct checklist and rarity meaning, not as ordinary set copies.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the Illustration Rare rarity label/symbol and the full-art scene treatment.
families.grookai_rule: Illustration Rare is a parent identity label when the printed card is stored as a distinct art/rarity lane.

families.why_it_exists: Rising Rivals includes Rotom-form cards with RT-prefixed card numbers.
families.why_collectors_care: The RT prefix identifies the Rotom-form subset lane and keeps these cards distinct from ordinary numbered Rising Rivals cards.
families.how_to_identify: Look for an RT-prefixed card number and Rotom form identity.
families.grookai_rule: RT-prefixed cards are parent identity lanes because the prefix changes printed checklist identity.

families.family_label: Base Pikachu Cheek / Shadowless Print Run
families.why_it_exists: Early Base Set Pikachu print runs differ by cheek color, edition, shadowless frame, and in one lane a weak/ghosted First Edition stamp impression.
families.why_collectors_care: These visual print-run differences identify early Base Set production states and are collected separately from later Unlimited copies.
families.how_to_identify: Check cheek color, shadowless frame, edition stamp, and whether the First Edition stamp is absent, normal, or ghosted.
families.grookai_rule: Visible print-run identity differences are parent identity variants, not finishes.

families.why_it_exists: Battle Academy products are beginner-friendly deck kits with fixed decks and teaching materials; some included cards carry deck/tutorial identity marks.
families.why_collectors_care: Deck-marked Battle Academy cards come from a specific teaching product and can be separated from normal set copies.
families.how_to_identify: Look for Battle Academy deck or sequence marks and confirm the source product year.
families.grookai_rule: Battle Academy deck-mark rows are parent identity variants when the mark is visible and source-backed.

families.why_it_exists: Pokémon Center stamped promos are tied to Pokémon Center direct retail or preorder campaigns for specific products.
families.why_collectors_care: The stamp identifies a direct-retail promotional copy that is physically distinct from an unstamped set or promo card.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the Pokémon Center stamp and verify the card against the exact campaign/product source.
families.grookai_rule: Pokémon Center stamps are parent identity variants; exact campaign text should be preserved whenever available.

families.why_it_exists: Detective Pikachu stamped promos were distributed through movie-era promotional channels, including stamped SM Promo variants tied to the POKEMON Detective Pikachu campaign.
families.why_collectors_care: The movie stamp identifies a campaign-distribution copy that collectors separate from the ordinary SM Promo or set card.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the Detective Pikachu movie stamp on the card face and confirm the promo number against card-specific checklist or marketplace references.
families.grookai_rule: Movie-campaign stamps are parent identity variants when the exact stamped card is source-backed.

families.why_it_exists: Selected Prismatic Evolutions Premium Collection products included stamped promotional reprints such as Lucario and Tyranitar collection cards.
families.why_collectors_care: The product stamp identifies a collection-box distribution copy, so collectors separate it from the unstamped Prismatic Evolutions card.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the Prismatic Evolutions stamp on the card face and verify the exact card against product announcement or catalog sources.
families.grookai_rule: Product-exclusive stamps are parent identity variants when exact product/card evidence is preserved.

families.why_it_exists: WB Kids stamped Wizards Black Star Promos were tied to Pokémon movie-era promotional distribution; missing and inverted stamp states are recognized special cases for specific cards.
families.why_collectors_care: The stamp state identifies how the promo copy was distributed or mis-stamped, creating a physically distinct lane from the ordinary Black Star Promo.
families.how_to_identify: Check whether the WB Kids stamp is present, missing, or inverted on the card face.
families.grookai_rule: Grookai models WB Kids stamp states as parent identity variants when card-specific evidence is strong enough.

families.why_it_exists: Base Set Pikachu has E3-stamped promotional versions; Bulbapedia records red-cheeked copies from Nintendo booth distribution at E3 in May 1999 and yellow-cheeked copies from Nintendo Power.
families.why_collectors_care: The stamp and cheek-color combination identifies a narrow early promotional distribution and separates these copies from ordinary Base Set Pikachu.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the gold E3 stamp and verify whether the Pikachu artwork has red or yellow cheeks.
families.grookai_rule: Grookai models the E3 stamp and cheek color together because both are visible identity dimensions.

families.why_it_exists: EB Games-stamped cards are retailer-exclusive promotional copies distributed through EB Games channels in markets where EB Games operates.
families.why_collectors_care: The EB Games stamp marks a regional retailer-exclusive copy, making it a distinct lane from the unstamped version of the same card.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the EB Games stamp and verify the exact card against retailer-exclusive product/checklist sources.
families.grookai_rule: EB Games stamps are parent identity variants when the exact card/stamp pairing is source-backed.

families.why_it_exists: World Championships stamped cards are tied to specific championship-event distributions or staff/placement programs.
families.why_collectors_care: The stamp connects the card to a specific competitive event and can encode staff, placement, or event participation context.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the World Championships, Worlds, Staff, or placement stamp on the card face.
families.grookai_rule: Worlds/event-stamped rows stay parent identity variants; exact event/card provenance should be preserved before adding new lanes.

families.why_it_exists: Asia Gym-stamped cards are regional event/distribution copies of existing cards, with the Gym mark applied to distinguish the promotional copy from the base set printing.
families.why_collectors_care: The Gym stamp marks a regional/event-distribution variant that collectors track separately from the ordinary set card and from Play! Pokémon Prize Pack stamped copies.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the Gym stamp on the card face and confirm the exact set, card number, and name against stamp-specific catalog references.
families.grookai_rule: Gym-stamped rows are parent identity variants when the exact card/stamp pairing is source-backed; the stamp is not modeled as a finish.

families.why_it_exists: First Edition copies were printed with an edition stamp identifying an earlier edition of the card.
families.why_collectors_care: The stamp marks a separate early print identity, usually collected separately from Unlimited copies.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the First Edition stamp on the card face.
families.grookai_rule: First Edition is modeled as a parent identity modifier, not as a finish.

families.why_it_exists: GameStop-stamped cards are retailer-exclusive promotional copies distributed through GameStop channels.
families.why_collectors_care: The GameStop stamp identifies a retailer-exclusive copy that collectors separate from unstamped promo or set copies.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the GameStop stamp and verify the exact card against retailer-exclusive product/checklist sources.
families.grookai_rule: GameStop stamps are parent identity variants when the exact card/stamp pairing is source-backed.

families.why_it_exists: JR East Stamp Rally promotional cards were tied to Japanese railway stamp-rally events and include visibly stamped promo copies.
families.why_collectors_care: The JR Stamp Rally mark ties the card to a narrow event-distribution context, making it distinct from the standard promo card.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the JR East Stamp Rally mark on the card face and verify the card against card-specific references.
families.grookai_rule: Stamp-rally event cards are parent identity variants when the exact card/stamp pairing is source-backed.

families.why_it_exists: The Pokémon Together stamp was used on Pikachu and Eevee promos distributed through Poké Post pop-up gift-pack campaigns.
families.why_collectors_care: The gold campaign stamp identifies the pop-up/gift-pack distribution copy rather than a normal set copy.
families.how_to_identify: Look for the Pokémon Together stamp on the card face.
families.grookai_rule: Campaign stamps are modeled as parent identities so the stamped copy can be owned, searched, and imaged separately.

families.family_label: Letter / Suffix Identity Needs Scope Decision
families.why_it_exists: This row uses a short variant/suffix token that appears to encode printed identity rather than a campaign stamp or special-origin story.
families.why_collectors_care: Some suffixes are collector-meaningful, but not every printed suffix needs a historical origin explanation.
families.how_to_identify: Review set, number, and active identity payload to decide whether this belongs in Variant Origin Index or a separate printed-identity explanation index.
families.grookai_rule: Do not publish a special-origin explanation until the lane is classified as stamp, error, print-run, or other collector-origin family.

families.why_it_exists: This parent row has a non-base variant/modifier, but the origin family is not yet mapped.
families.why_collectors_care: It may still be a legitimate collector lane, but Grookai should not publish origin copy until the family is reviewed.
families.how_to_identify: Review the visible card, variant key, printed identity modifier, and original evidence source.
families.grookai_rule: Unclassified rows stay canonical if already reconciled, but public origin text is blocked.

sample_cards.name: Team Galactic's Invention G-107 Technical Machine G
